title: Mathematics for Machine Learning
subtitle: A Comprehensive Guide to the Mathematical Foundations of ML
keyIdeas: Linear algebra is the language of data: vectors encode examples, matrices encode transformations, and tensor operations underlie every neural network forward pass
Analytic geometry reveals the geometry of high-dimensional spaces — inner products, norms, orthogonal projections, and rotations — that make geometric intuition precise
Matrix decompositions (eigendecomposition, SVD, Cholesky) expose latent structure in data and power algorithms from PCA to recommender systems
Vector calculus provides the gradient, Jacobian, and Hessian — the tools that make differentiable programming possible and backpropagation derivable
Probability quantifies uncertainty: random variables, Bayes' rule, and estimators (MLE, MAP) form the foundation of statistical learning
Continuous optimization turns learning into a numerical problem: gradient descent, stochastic methods, and constrained optimization are the engines of model fitting
Mathematical maturity is a prerequisite for genuine ML understanding — libraries hide the math but not the assumptions, the failure modes, or the limits
keyTakeaways: Vectors, matrices, and tensor operations are not abstractions — they are the literal data structures flowing through every model
Eigendecomposition characterizes linear maps; SVD generalizes to non-square matrices and is the workhorse of dimensionality reduction
The gradient of a scalar loss points in the direction of steepest ascent; gradient descent follows the negative gradient to find minima
Jacobians generalize gradients to vector-valued functions and are the building block of the chain rule (backpropagation)
Maximum likelihood estimation finds parameters that maximize the probability of observed data; MAP adds a prior to regularize
Convex problems have a unique global minimum that gradient methods can find efficiently; non-convex problems (deep learning) require care
Constrained optimization via Lagrange multipliers extends calculus to problems with equality and inequality constraints
The book deliberately avoids ML applications until the final worked-examples chapter — the math must stand on its own
whoShouldRead: Undergraduate and graduate students in machine learning, data science, or applied mathematics needing a unified math foundation
ML practitioners who learned frameworks by imitation and want to understand the derivations behind gradient descent, PCA, and backpropagation
Self-taught engineers whose math has gaps in linear algebra, vector calculus, or probability and want a single rigorous reference
Researchers transitioning into ML from physics, engineering, or statistics who need the conventions of the ML community explained
Educators designing an ML math curriculum and looking for a textbook that builds from first principles
whoShouldSkip: Readers seeking hands-on ML code — this is a math textbook; the single worked-examples chapter is the only ML application
Practitioners needing production deep learning guidance — the book predates transformers and contains no coverage of modern architectures
Anyone looking for a quick, non-technical overview — every page requires working through derivations
Readers whose goal is to pass a coding interview — this book builds deep understanding, not short-term recall tricks
Statisticians who already master probability and linear algebra at a graduate level will find the coverage survey-level

relatedBooks.title: Deep Learning
relatedBooks.author: Ian Goodfellow, Yoshua Bengio, Aaron Courville
relatedBooks.reason: The canonical deep learning textbook; assumes the linear algebra, probability, and calculus foundations that this book builds from scratch

relatedBooks.title: Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning
relatedBooks.author: Christopher Bishop
relatedBooks.reason: A more comprehensive ML text with the same mathematical seriousness; the natural next step after mastering this book's foundations

relatedBooks.title: Introduction to Linear Algebra
relatedBooks.author: Gilbert Strang
relatedBooks.reason: The classic linear algebra text; deeper on the geometry and intuition that this book condenses into a few chapters

relatedBooks.title: Convex Optimization
relatedBooks.author: Stephen Boyd & Lieven Vandenberghe
relatedBooks.reason: The standard reference for the optimization chapter; covers convex analysis in far greater depth with engineering applications

relatedBooks.title: Probabilistic Machine Learning
relatedBooks.author: Kevin P. Murphy
relatedBooks.reason: An ML textbook that treats probability as the unifying framework; deepens this book's probability chapter with Bayesian modeling

relatedBooks.title: The Matrix Calculus You Need For Deep Learning
relatedBooks.author: Terence Parr & Jeremy Howard
relatedBooks.reason: A short, focused companion that derives the gradient rules used in backpropagation; useful supplement to the vector calculus chapter

relatedBooks.title: Information Theory, Inference, and Learning Algorithms
relatedBooks.author: David MacKay
relatedBooks.reason: A free, idiosyncratic masterpiece that connects probability, information theory, and learning — the philosophical complement to this book's formalism

editions.notes: First edition, published 2020. Free PDF draft available at mml-book.github.io.
Second edition, published December 2023. Refined based on classroom use at UCL, ETH, and partner institutions.

awards: Free PDF openly available at mml-book.github.io
Adopted at UCL Centre for AI, ETH Zürich, TU Munich, and dozens of graduate ML programs worldwide
Average rating 4.5+ on Amazon and Goodreads (hundreds of reviews)
excerpt: We wrote this book because the mathematical foundations of machine learning are increasingly important to practitioners, yet they are often taught in disconnected fragments across courses on linear algebra, calculus, probability, and optimization. This book unifies the four pillars into a single coherent narrative: the math you need to read, understand, and contribute to modern machine learning research — without drowning in excessive formalism.
